primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cutaneous lupus flare (photosensitive malar rash), no internal organ involvement

pathophysiology_summary: Photosensitive cutaneous lupus produces the classic malar ('butterfly') rash — a well-demarcated erythematous eruption over the cheeks and nasal bridge that characteristically SPARES the nasolabial folds — triggered by UV exposure in ANA-positive patients. It is a skin manifestation and does not itself indicate internal organ involvement. The alarming burning erythema raises fear of facial cellulitis/erysipelas (which involve the nasolabial folds, are usually unilateral, warm, indurated and febrile), allergic contact dermatitis (weeping eczema) and a systemic lupus crisis. The exclusions — cellulitis and organ crisis (serositis: pleuritic pain/breathlessness; nephritis: cola urine/oedema) — are screenable by history/appearance and absent here. Management is cool compress, mild topical corticosteroid, strict photoprotection and an organ-crisis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Sun-triggered bilateral malar erythema sparing the nasolabial folds in an ANA-positive patient.
Screen for nasolabial involvement, unilaterality, spreading induration, fever; absent.
Screen for pleuritic pain/breathlessness/cola urine; absent.
No weeping eczema, no telangiectasia/papulopustules; sun-triggered butterfly pattern favours lupus.
Nasolabial-sparing bilateral rash, afebrile, no induration/pus (DI-001), no serositis/nephritis (DI-002), sun-triggered on positive ANA (DI-003) — a cutaneous flare without organ involvement.
Nasolabial sparing, bilateral, afebrile — cellulitis not supported (safety-net retained).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: cool compress + mild topical corticosteroid + strict photoprotection, reassure no organ involvement, routine GP/rheumatology review (± hydroxychloroquine), and an organ-crisis safety-net. ED over-triage of an afebrile cutaneous flare is unnecessary.

red_flags.description: Facial cellulitis/erysipelas — nasolabial involvement/unilateral spreading warm induration with fever
Systemic lupus crisis — serositis (pleuritic pain/breathlessness) or nephritis (cola urine/oedema)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating a nasolabial-sparing bilateral malar rash as cellulitis with antibiotics/ED without checking for fever/induration/unilaterality.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Assuming a skin flare = organ crisis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reassuring or escalating without screening the actual serositis/nephritis features.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Cool compress + mild topical corticosteroid + strict photoprotection with an organ-crisis safety-net; routine GP/rheumatology review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging an afebrile cutaneous flare wastes resources; missing facial cellulitis or a genuine organ crisis would be dangerous (hence the retained safety-nets).
